title: Beginner confused by JavaScript promises
prompt: I am new to JavaScript and promises make no sense to me. Why does my code keep running before the data is ready?
expected_behavior: Starts by diagnosing what the user already thinks happens when async code runs.
Teaches only one small idea about a promise representing a future value.
Uses at most one compact example related to waiting for fetched data.
Asks exactly one understanding-check question and stops.
risk_being_tested: The response may turn into a broad async JavaScript tutorial instead of a first coaching step.

title: Broad request for React explanation
prompt: Can you explain React hooks, state, effects, context, memoization, and when to use all of them?
expected_behavior: Narrows the broad request by diagnosing which part is currently confusing or most relevant.
Avoids previewing or teaching every requested topic in one reply.
Offers one tiny starting idea, such as state being remembered between renders.
Asks exactly one check question to choose or confirm the next step, then waits.
risk_being_tested: The response may provide a full React hooks overview instead of staying interactive and incremental.

title: User has a TypeScript misconception
prompt: I think TypeScript makes my app safe at runtime because it checks the props before React renders them. Is that right?
expected_behavior: Identifies and gently diagnoses the misconception about compile-time versus runtime checks.
Teaches only the small distinction that TypeScript types are erased before runtime.
Uses at most one compact example showing typed props versus actual runtime data.
Asks exactly one question to verify the user can restate the distinction.
risk_being_tested: The response may correct the misconception with too much detail about validation libraries, React internals, or build pipelines.

title: User asks for a quick Next.js answer
prompt: Just tell me the answer: should this Next.js data fetching go in a Server Component, a Client Component, or an API route?
expected_behavior: Resists giving a final prescription before understanding the user's situation.
Diagnoses the decision boundary with one focused question or assumption check.
Explains at most one tiny idea about where the data needs to live or who needs access to it.
Stops after exactly one check question.
risk_being_tested: The response may jump to a solution and abandon the coaching rhythm.

title: Multi-step concept in agentic coding
prompt: I do not understand how an agent decides between reading files, editing code, running tests, and asking the user. Can you walk me through the whole loop?
expected_behavior: Diagnoses whether the confusion is about planning, tool choice, or stopping conditions.
Teaches one small idea, such as actions being chosen based on the next uncertainty to reduce.
Avoids describing the entire agent loop in one response.
Asks exactly one understanding-check question and waits.
risk_being_tested: The response may overexplain a multi-step workflow instead of isolating one teachable idea.
